articles.title: Remedies for a Constitutional Crisis
articles.content:
在宪法对决中，司法部门似乎只有有限的救济手段。一旦走到法院认定行政分支违法的那一步，除了命令其遵守法律，他们还能做什么？而我们除了希望行政部门受到良心或政治力量的约束而被迫遵守之外，又能做什么？

如今，这种遵守已不能想当然。事实上，风险在于行政部门会无视司法机构——忽视其判决、拒绝服从其命令、甘愿承受藐视法庭的指控、诬告法官虚构错误行为，并暗中鼓励对法官的报复。确实，有证据表明这正在发生。

结果是，我们正陷入司法权威的危机——可以称之为“宪法危机”。我们不情愿地使用这个术语，因为它被过度使用且含糊不清。而且我们在使用它时并不知道接下来会发生什么，即使情况恶化可能需要一个新的词汇。（它被称为“古巴导弹危机”，尽管结果平安无事，尽管可能变得更糟，尽管如果真的恶化，我们需要一个新术语来描述所发生的事。）“宪法危机”这个短语至少捕捉到了法院今天面临异常挑战的感觉，而且先例——如果存在的话——并不令人安心。无论你怎么称呼，这不是好事。

法院在这样的危机中能做什么来执行其判决，正是本文的核心问题。如果法院不能假设行政部门会简单接受其判决，那么最不危险的部门还有什么选择？联邦法院是否有办法促使行政部门表现得更加守法？或者这些选择是否会加速滑向一种均衡——行政分支表现得藐视法庭却逃脱惩罚？联邦法院是否有办法有效抵抗行政分支的违法行径、支撑法治？还是这些选择只会加速并加剧部门间的冲突，引发一场司法部门可以发起但无法获胜的战斗？

我们的核心主张是，法院可用的救济选择范围比乍看之下更广泛——而最佳路径并不总是显而易见的。即使行政分支的行动明显违法，法院能做什么并不一定清楚。即使我们正处于危机中，司法应对的选择非常重要，这也不会使正确答案变得显而易见。我们认识到，读者会以各种不同的方式评估每种选择的风险，我们自己的评估也可能随着世界的变化而改变。（事实上，自本文于2025年10月发表以来，情况已发生很大变化。）因此，本文并不试图将任何一种选择冠以正确之名。我们也不声称这些救济选择中的任何一个足以避免或化解宪法危机。从这个意义上说，它们并非真正的救济手段。

我们的目标更为谦逊：我们将列出联邦法官可用的选项菜单，突出它们之间的权衡。但我们认识到，我们不承担选择的重担——这一重担属于法官——而且我们应当承认，也属于总统、其他行政官员和雇员，以及国会议员。在每一次三权分立的危机中，在每一次被化解的三权分立危机中，决定性的决策并非由法学教授做出：

斗牛批评家排排坐
挤满巨大的斗牛场；
但只有一人知晓真相
而他就是那个斗牛士。

全文见PDF全文

本文最初发表于《哈佛法律评论》
articles.summary:
Introduction

In a constitutional showdown with the executive branch, the courts may seem to have limited remedial options. Once we reach a point where courts conclude that the Executive is violating the law, what can they do but order compliance? And what can we do but hope that the Executive feels compelled to comply, whether by conscience or political forces?

Today, that compliance is hard to take for granted. Indeed, the risk is that the executive branch will blow off the judiciary, ignoring its judgments, refusing to obey its decrees, courting contempt, accusing judges of fictitious wrongdoing, and tacitly encouraging retaliation against them. And indeed, there is evidence that this is what is happening.

The upshot is that we are slipping into a crisis of judicial authority — what one could call a “constitutional crisis.” We use the phrase here reluctantly, for it is overused and vague. And we use it without knowing what will come next, and even though we might need a new word if things get worse. (It was called the “Cuban Missile Crisis,” even though it ended fine, and even though it could have gotten much worse, and even though we would have needed a new term for what happened if it did.) The phrase “constitutional crisis” at least captures the sense that the courts face an unusual challenge today, and that the precedents, such as they are, are not reassuring. Call it what you will, it is not good.

What courts can do to enforce their decisions in such a crisis is the inquiry at the heart of this Essay. If courts cannot assume that the Executive will simply accept their decisions, what options are open to the least dangerous branch? Do the federal courts have choices that will coax the executive branch into more law-abiding behavior? Or will those choices hasten the slide toward an equilibrium in which the Executive acts contemptuously but escapes contempt? Do the federal courts have choices that will provide an effective resistance to lawbreaking by the Executive, shoring up the rule of law? Or will those choices merely accelerate and heighten the conflict between the branches, precipitating a battle that the judiciary can start but not win?

Our central claim is that the range of remedial options available to courts is broader than it may seem at first — and that the best path is not always obvious. Even when it is clear that the executive branch has acted unlawfully, it is not necessarily clear what courts can do about it. Even if we are in a crisis, and the choice of judicial response is very important, that does not make obvious the correct choice. We recognize that our readers will assess the risks associated with each option in various and varying ways, and that our own assessments may change as the world changes. (Indeed, much has changed since this Essay was presented in October 2025.) So this Essay does not attempt to crown any one choice as the right one. Nor do we claim that any of these remedial choices will be enough to avert or undo a constitutional crisis. They are not remedies in that sense.

Our aim is more modest: We will lay out the menu of options that are available to federal judges, highlighting the tradeoffs among them. But we recognize that we do not have the burden of choice, which belongs instead to the judges — and, we should acknowledge, to the President and the other executive officers and employees, as well as to the members of Congress. In every separation of powers crisis, and in every averted separation of powers crisis, the decisive decisions are not made by law professors:

Bullfight critics ranked in rows

Crowd the enormous Plaza full;

But only one is there who knows

And he’s the man who fights the bull.

Continue Reading in the Full PDF

The post Remedies for a Constitutional Crisis appeared first on Harvard Law Review.

articles.title: The Supreme Court Under Threat: Early Lessons in Judicial Self-Protection
articles.content:
由于最高法院拥有强大权力，它在很大程度上能够履行其法律职责。然而，由于它是一个法院——由于它既没有剑也没有钱袋——它容易遭到总统和各州的成功违抗，也容易受到国会的惩罚。

本文认为，最高法院之所以在脆弱中变得强大，是因为它通过艰难的实践学会了相当好地扮演两种角色。第一个是法律角色：法院在审理的案件中解释和适用宪法及联邦法规。第二个是政治角色：法院必须在一个最终必须说服有权势的政客遵守其裁决的世界中生存。简而言之，法院有两个核心任务，尤其是在受到威胁时——维护法治，以及保护自身。认为法院应仅专注于其法律功能的评论者未能充分认识到，即使在今天，法院不仅在行动，也会被施加行动——或者被忽视。

正如本文所示，法院很早就开始扮演这两种角色。法院按照宪法第三条和1789年《司法法案》的指示，提供了法律解释和适用的服务。但法院很快认识到，如果不能发展出一种自我保护的政治实践方式，就无法继续履行这一法律职能。通过几个历史事件的分析，本文认为最高法院在行使司法审查权时，不可避免地且（在一定限度内）适当地考虑了对其自身的政治威胁。

第一部分确定了法院制度脆弱性的原因，以及法院为应对困难局面而逐步形成的应对机制，包括当前面临的挑战。第二至第四部分考察了十九世纪的三个关键时刻，法院在这些时刻成功地——虽然并非总是完美地——应对了对其持续权威的生存威胁：1800年选举后杰斐逊派对司法机构的反弹；1830年代乔治亚州在切罗基案件中对法院的违抗；以及内战后就军事审判的法院裁决。结论部分为法院使用司法自我保护工具的行为进行了辩护，尽管这种使用存在潜在成本。

“面临威胁的最高法院：司法自我保护的早期教训”首发于哈佛法律评论。
articles.summary:
Because the Supreme Court is powerful, it is largely able to fulfill its legal responsibilities. Because it is a court — because it lacks both the sword and the purse — it is vulnerable to being defied successfully by Presidents and states and punished by Congress.

This Essay argues that the Court has become powerful despite being vulnerable because it has learned through hard experience to perform two roles reasonably well. The first is legal: The Court interprets and applies the Constitution and federal statutes in disputes before it. The second is political: The Court must survive in a world in which it ultimately must persuade powerful politicians to abide by its decisions. In short, the Court has two central tasks, especially when under threat — preserving the rule of law, and protecting itself. Commentators who believe that the Court should focus exclusively on its legal function fail to credit sufficiently the fact that, even today, the Court does not just act; it can also be acted upon — or ignored.

As this Essay shows, the Court began playing both roles early on. The Court made itself available to interpret and apply the law, as Article III of the Constitution and the Judiciary Act of 1789 directed. But the Court soon learned that it could not continue performing this legal function if it did not develop a way of practicing self-protective politics. Drawing upon several historical episodes, this Essay contends that the Supreme Court inevitably, and (within limits) properly, takes account of political threats to itself in exercising judicial review.

Part I identifies the reasons for the Court’s institutional vulnerability and the devices that the Court has developed over time to navigate difficult situations, including the current challenges facing the Court. Parts II through IV examine three episodes from the nineteenth century in which the Court successfully, although not always perfectly, navigated existential threats to its continued authority: cases arising out of the Jeffersonian backlash against the judiciary after the elections of 1800; Georgia’s defiance of the Court in the Cherokee Cases of the 1830s; and the Court’s decisions about military trials after the Civil War. The Conclusion defends the Court’s use of the tools of judicial self-protection despite the potential costs of such use.

Continue Reading in the Full PDF

The post The Supreme Court Under Threat: Early Lessons in Judicial Self-Protection appeared first on Harvard Law Review.

articles.title: Aggregation and the “Universal” Injunction
articles.content:
司法审查的关键功能不仅是“宣示法律是什么”，还包括提供救济手段以维护法律尊严。当政府官员的行为违反联邦法律（包括违反宪法）时，司法审查发挥着尤为重要的作用。当政府官员违法时，司法审查功能支持一种救济方式，即要求官员停止追求非法政策或做法，从而遵守联邦法律。

此类“合规”禁令被称为“普遍”或“全国性”禁令，因为其范围超出了“向法庭上的原告提供完全救济”的范畴。这种表述方式将合规禁令定性为聚合工具——即“不当试图'创造事实上的集体诉讼'”。尽管这种定性从未完全准确，但特朗普诉CASA公司案中的多数意见采纳了这一观点，并因此狭义解释联邦法院在个人原告提起的案件中发布此类禁令的法定权力。CASA判决使我们必须通过聚合的视角来审视“普遍”禁令——具体而言，通过《联邦民事诉讼规则》第23条规定的集体诉讼视角来审视。

本文首先简要总结CASA判决关于“普遍”禁令可获得性的裁判要旨。第二部分讨论救济与司法审查之间的关系，以及此类禁令在其中的重要作用。第三部分探讨集体诉讼如何成为联邦法院在开展司法审查后，使其裁判产生实际效力的一种替代路径——即要求官员遵守法律并停止非法行为或政策。我们还回应了在此情形下使用集体诉讼的几个异议。最后一部分讨论集体诉讼途径对“普遍”禁令的一些影响，指出集体诉讼与此类禁令之间的核心功能联系，同时也承认这一集体诉讼途径可能带来的问题。
articles.summary:
Introduction

A crucial function of judicial review is not only “to say what the law is,” but also to provide the remedies to vindicate that law. Judicial review plays an especially important role when government officials act in ways that violate federal law, including when they violate the Constitution. When government officials violate the law, the judicial review function supports a remedy that requires officials to comply with federal law by ceasing to pursue the illegal policy or practice.

Such “compliance” injunctions have been given the label “universal” or “nationwide” injunctions insofar as they go beyond giving “complete relief to the plaintiffs before the court.” Among other things, this framing couches compliance injunctions as aggregation devices — as improper attempts to “create de facto class actions.” Although this characterization has never been entirely accurate, the majority in Trump v. CASA, Inc. embraced it and, as a result, narrowly construed the federal courts’ statutory authority to issue such injunctions in cases brought by individual plaintiffs. The CASA decision makes it imperative to reckon with “universal” injunctions through the lens of aggregation — specifically, through the lens of class actions under Rule 23 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure.

In this Essay, we begin with a brief summary of the CASA decision’s holding regarding the availability of “universal” injunctions. Part II discusses the relationship between remedies and judicial review and the important role that such injunctions play in that regard. Part III explores how class actions could serve as an alternative path for federal courts, after engaging in judicial review, to make their decisions meaningful by requiring officials to comply with the law and to cease illegal actions or policies. We also respond to several objections to the use of class actions in this context. The final Part addresses some implications of the class action route to “universal” injunctions, identifying the core functional connection between class actions and such injunctions, but also acknowledging some of the problems that this class action route may pose.

Continue Reading in the Full PDF

The post Aggregation and the “Universal” Injunction appeared first on Harvard Law Review.

articles.title: Exploring the Limits of Qualified Immunity Under Harlow’s Discretionary Function Test
articles.content:
在哈洛诉菲茨杰拉德案这一最高法院关于有限豁免权的主要判例中，隐藏着一个几乎被忽视的短语，它援引了官方责任与政府问责法中一个几乎被遗忘的区分。哈洛案因法院那句经常被引用的有限豁免权测试标准而闻名：“因此，我们裁定，履行裁量职能的政府官员 generally免于承担民事赔偿责任，只要其行为不违反明文确立的法定或宪法权利，而理性之人本应知晓这些权利。”后续许多 doctrine 和评论都聚焦于哈洛法院自觉决定修改有限豁免权的主观“善意”要素，以“明文确立的法律”要求取而代之。很少有人关注——实际上几乎无人关注——法院决定将这一新标准的适用范围限制在对履行“裁量职能”的官员提起的诉讼中，而将履行“行政”任务的官员排除在豁免权保护之外。此后法院仅在哈洛案之后寥寥数次提及这一区分，但从未阐述行政职能与裁量职能之间的差异如何与有限豁免权 doctrine 相关联。学者们尚未对这一问题进行任何详细研究。

由于最高法院未就行政职能与裁量职能的区分提供指导，也未给出后来法官可能用来划定 operative 分界线的明确理由，大多数下级法院本能地将有限豁免权法律扩展至涵盖所有官方行为。因此，“明文确立的法律”标准 seemingly 适用于所有宪法侵权诉讼，无论它们涉及高级政府官员的裁量行为和思维过程，还是最好被理解为行政职能的基层官员行为。

在本文中，我们旨在挖掘哈洛案所依赖的行政职能与裁量职能之间这一长期存在的区分，并将其应用于有限豁免权 doctrine。我们分三部分进行。第一部分概述了十九世纪政府问责法如何通过两种方式保护法治价值：一方面 shielding 高级官员行为，另一方面授权对低级官员提起诉讼以 preserve 法治价值。十九世纪法律通过将诉讼指向履行行政职能的行政官员来实现这一点。在包括 trespass 令状、mandamus 令和 habeas corpus 人身保护令在内的各种救济形式中，审查政府政策合法性的诉讼通常针对基层官员而非 policymaking 官员。这类行政行为人不得享有诉讼豁免权，也不能以服从上级命令作为个人责任的抗辩。赔偿金减轻了个人责任的刺痛，并将不合规成本转移给了政府。

在澄清行政问责在十九世纪的作用后，第二部分解释了宪法诉讼在二十世纪如何发生变化。common law 中的裁量职能 exemption 在官员可以 free from 任何责任威胁的裁量范围内行事。但在 Ex parte Young 案一个 mostly 被忽视的要素中，最高法院驳回了以下主张：作为行使执法裁量权的高级政府官员，明尼苏达州司法部长爱德华·T·杨的起诉决定不受司法控制。为得出这一结论，法院认为第十四修正案对杨施加了一项义务，即不得执行违宪的州法律，并 override 杨根据州法律可能享有的任何官方豁免权。后来的判例扩大了宪法权利的直接执行，为针对高级官员的宪法侵权诉讼奠定了基础，并对政策制定构成了威胁，而法院选择用有限豁免权来 manage 这一威胁。

第三部分从法治角度解释为什么哈洛法院对高级裁量行为人的保护并不 inevitably 扩展至执行相关政策的基层官员。根据十九世纪的模式，哈洛案平衡了保护高级官员的重要性与通过针对低级或行政行为人的诉讼 preserve 合法性测试的需要。在政府官员通过简易行动执法、不存在替代审查模式、以及 state of mind 和政策制定在合法性评估中不构成干扰因素的地方，个人应当有权在不克服哈洛案明确法律标准的情况下 test 宪法问题。事实上，许多州继续在责任方面区分行政职能与裁量职能，赞同哈洛案的基本直觉——豁免权不延伸至所有官方行为。第三部分表明，任何 plausible 版本的行政职能-裁量职能区分——无论根植于十九世纪还是更近期的 doctrine——都将缩小有限豁免权的 footprint 并减轻其众多 well-documented 问题的冲击。简短的结论 follows。

继续阅读完整 PDF
这篇博文《探索哈洛案裁量职能测试下有限豁免权的限度》首次发表于《哈佛法律评论》。
articles.summary:
Introduction

Buried in Harlow v. Fitzgerald, the Supreme Court’s leading decision on qualified immunity, a largely overlooked phrase invokes a mostly forgotten distinction in the law of official liability and government accountability.  Harlow is well known for the Court’s oft-quoted test for qualified immunity: “We therefore hold that government officials performing discretionary functions, generally are shielded from liability for civil damages insofar as their conduct does not violate clearly established statutory or constitutional rights of which a reasonable person would have known.” Much subsequent doctrine and commentary has focused on the Harlow Court’s self-conscious decision to modify qualified immunity’s subjective “good faith” element, putting a “clearly established law” requirement in its place.  Much less attention — indeed, almost no attention at all — has been paid to the Court’s decision to confine application of this new standard to claims brought against officers performing “discretionary functions,” leaving officers performing “‘ministerial’ tasks” unprotected by immunity.  The Court itself has adverted to the distinction only a few times since Harlow, but has never outlined the difference between ministerial and discretionary functions as it relates to qualified immunity doctrine. Scholars have yet to consider the matter in any detail.

With no guidance from the Court distinguishing ministerial from discretionary tasks, and no clearly stated rationale that later jurists might use to draw the operative line, most lower courts have reflexively extended the law of qualified immunity to encompass all official action. As a consequence, the “clearly established law” standard seemingly applies to all constitutional tort claims, whether they implicate the discretionary acts and thought processes of high-level government officials or the actions of line officers best understood as ministerial.

In this Essay, we aim both to excavate the longstanding distinction between ministerial and discretionary functions on which Harlow relied and to bring it to bear on qualified immunity doctrine. We do so in three Parts. Part I sketches how nineteenth-century government accountability law both shielded high-level official action and preserved rule of law values by authorizing suit to go forward against low-level officials. It did so by directing litigation at executive officials performing ministerial functions. Across a range of remedial forms — including those initiated by writs of trespass, mandamus, and habeas corpus — suits to test the legality of government policy were typically directed at line officers rather than policymaking officials. Such ministerial actors enjoyed no immunity from suit and could not plead superior orders as a defense to personal liability. Indemnity took the sting out of personal liability and shifted the cost of noncompliance to the government.

Having clarified the role of ministerial accountability in the nineteenth century, Part II explains how constitutional litigation changed in the twentieth. The discretionary function exemption operated at common law to create a zone of discretion within which the official could act free from any threat of liability. But in a mostly overlooked element of Ex parte Young, the Supreme Court rejected the claim that, as a high government official exercising discretion as to the enforcement of law, Minnesota Attorney General Edward T. Young’s prosecutorial decisions lay beyond judicial control. In reaching that conclusion, the Court viewed the Fourteenth Amendment as imposing a duty on Young to refrain from enforcing unconstitutional state laws and as overriding any official immunity that Young might enjoy under state law. Later decisions broadened the direct enforcement of constitutional rights, setting the stage for constitutional tort suits against high-level officials and posing a threat to policy formulation that the Court chose to manage with qualified immunity.

Part III explains on rule of law grounds why the Harlow Court’s protections for high-level discretionary actors do not inevitably extend to line officers who carry out the policies in question. In keeping with the nineteenth-century model, Harlow balanced the importance of protecting high-level officials with the need to preserve a test of legality through litigation directed at low-level or ministerial actors. Where government officials enforce the law through summary action, where no alternative modes of review exist, and where state of mind and policy formation play no confounding role in the assessment of legality, individuals should have a right to test constitutionality without having to surmount Harlow’s clear-law standard. Indeed, many states continue to distinguish between ministerial and discretionary functions for liability purposes, agreeing with Harlow’s animating intuition that immunity does not extend to all official conduct. Part III shows that any plausible version of the ministerial-discretionary distinction — whether rooted in nineteenth-century or more recent doctrine — would shrink qualified immunity’s footprint and lessen the impact of its many well-documented problems. A brief conclusion follows.

Continue Reading in the Full PDF

The post Exploring the Limits of Qualified Immunity Under <em>Harlow</em>’s Discretionary Function Test appeared first on Harvard Law Review.

articles.title: Some Realism About Constitutional Remedies
articles.content:
最高法院在处理行政权力问题时一直在两种方法之间摇摆不定，这两种方法通常被称为制度形式主义和制度现实主义。形式主义将总统这样的机构视为一个“黑箱”，宪法赋予它某些权力。例如在“特朗普诉夏威夷案”中，最高法院支持了特朗普总统臭名昭著的旅行禁令，其关注点不在于“特定的总统”及其过去呼吁的“全面禁止穆斯林进入美国”，而在于“总统职位本身的权威”。这就是制度形式主义的语言。

另一方面，现实主义则审视“黑箱”内部，在定义机构权力之前考虑它实际上如何运作以及由谁组成。罗伯茨法院有时也展现出更具现实主义取向的迹象。例如在“商务部诉纽约州案”中，法院驳斥了特朗普政府关于在人口普查中增加国籍问题的解释，指出“我们‘不需要表现出普通公民所不必具备的天真’”。这就是制度现实主义的语言。

两种方法都各有其吸引力。一方面，面对总统的不当行为，形式主义可能显得轻信且无能为力。另一方面，法院必须制定适用于现任政府离任后很长时间的总统权力规则。无论人们对这场关于总统权力的法学争论持何种立场，我们在这篇文章中认为，在为行政部门制定救济措施时，适度采用现实主义方法是完全适当的。毕竟，救济措施必然横跨“理想与现实之间”。它们旨在在现实世界中实现宪法价值。为实现这一目标，宪法救济措施应考虑其适用对象的本届政府的性质和倾向。

本文分为三个部分。在第一部分中，我们解释为什么行政部门“自我约束”地适用法律限制——无需法院介入——对法治至关重要。然后我们梳理证据，说明当前总统及其政府缺乏自我约束的精神。第二部分探讨当前困扰联邦法院的两个救济问题。首先，政府取消了向广泛接受者提供的拨款，明显违反了现行法规和先前承诺的资金。其次，政府声称已将众多官员从独立机构中移除，违反了法定任期保护规定。在对这些激进举措提起的诉讼中，联邦法院面临救济方式的选择：受伤方是否可以在地区法院寻求禁令以撤销或终止违法行为，还是必须向联邦索赔法院（CFC）寻求资金损失或欠薪的损害赔偿。我们认为，根据现行法规和先例，地区法院的禁令救济或其功能等同物在两种情况下均可获得。

综合前两个部分，第三部分认为，衡平法救济必须今天就可以获得，以弥补行政官员缺乏“自我约束”的状况。仅仅依靠金钱救济不足以约束由霍姆斯所说的“坏人”组成的行政部门——即那些只将“法律”视为对法院将强制他们做什么的预测、而不听从“良心上更模糊的制裁”的人。如果一个“坏人”行政首长只需面对损害赔偿，即使法院最终裁定其行为违法，它仍可实现其所有目标。

进入衡平法的领域。衡平法的两个传统功能是在法律救济不足时介入，以及防止投机行为。如果衡平法救济不能实际上使坏人变好，它至少可以迫使他们遵守更高的标准。用实际的话说，“衡平法通常会命令去做那些本应已经做的事。”当面对坏人时，这应当成为法院救济的指导原则。当然，禁令并非在每个案件中都必须发出，但根据传统衡平法原则，它至少应当可以获得，既作为对行政部门的外部约束，也为了加强行政部门内部呼吁克制的任何声音。

归根结底，某些司法救济手段维护法治的充分性，取决于行政部门内部的法治文化。当官员们不致力于这种文化时，需要更有力的衡平法救济来保护所涉及的潜在宪法原则。而法院今天在制定救济措施以使行政部门保持在法律范围内时，应当对当前本届政府的性质采取一些现实主义态度。
articles.summary:
Introduction

The Supreme Court has wavered between two approaches to questions of executive power, which are often labeled institutional formalism and realism. Formalism treats an institution like the presidency as a “black box” to which the Constitution assigns certain powers. In Trump v. Hawaii, for example, the Supreme Court upheld President Trump’s infamous travel ban by focusing not on the “particular President” and his past call for a “complete shutdown of Muslims entering the United States,” but rather on “the authority of the Presidency itself.” That is the language of institutional formalism.

Realism, on the other hand, peers into the “black box,” taking account of how an institution actually works and who actually populates it before defining its powers. At times, the Roberts Court has shown glimmers of a more realist orientation. In Department of Commerce v. New York, for instance, the Court discredited the Trump Administration’s explanation for why it added a citizenship question to the census by noting that “we are ‘not required to exhibit a naiveté from which ordinary citizens are free.’” That is the language of institutional realism.

One can appreciate the allure of both approaches. On the one hand, formalism may feel credulous and impotent in the face of presidential misconduct. On the other hand, the Court must craft rules of presidential power that will apply long after the incumbent administration has departed. Wherever one stands on this jurisprudential debate about presidential power, though, we argue in this Essay that a dose of realism is entirely appropriate when it comes to crafting remedies for the executive branch. Remedies, after all, necessarily straddle “the ideal and the real.” They aim to effectuate constitutional values in the world as it is. To achieve that aim, constitutional remedies should take account of the nature and propensities of the administration to which they will be addressed.

The Essay has three Parts. In the first Part, we explain why it is generally important to the rule of law that the executive branch “self-apply” legal limits without the involvement of the courts. We then canvass the evidence that the current President and his Administration lack an ethos of self-application. Part II explores two remedial questions now vexing the federal courts. First, the Administration has canceled grants to a broad array of recipients in plain violation of governing statutes and prior obligations of funds. Second, the Administration has purported to remove numerous officials from independent agencies in violation of statutory removal protections. In the ensuing challenges to these aggressive actions, the federal courts have faced a choice of remedy: whether an injured party may seek an injunction in district court to undo or to end the unlawful action, or must instead pursue a damages remedy for lost funds or backpay in the Court of Federal Claims (CFC). We argue that injunctive relief or its functional equivalent in a district court is available in both contexts under existing statutes and precedents.

Putting the first two Parts together, Part III argues that equitable remedies must be available today to make up for a lack of “self-application” by executive officials. Monetary remedies alone are inadequate to constrain an executive branch populated by Holmesian “bad” men — that is, people who view the “law” only as a prediction of what a court will force them to do and who do not heed “the vaguer sanctions of conscience.” A “bad man” Executive facing only damages could achieve all its aims even if the courts ultimately adjudicate its actions to be illegal.

Enter equity. Two of the traditional functions of equity are to step in when legal remedies are inadequate and to prevent opportunism. If

an equitable remedy cannot make bad actors good in fact, it can at least force them to abide by a better standard. In practical terms, “generally equity will order to be done that which ought to have been done.” That should be the courts’ remedial lodestar when confronted with a bad man. An injunction, of course, does not need to be issued in every case, but it should at least be available in accord with traditional equitable principles, both as an external check on the Executive and to fortify any voices within the Executive calling for restraint.

In the end, the adequacy of certain judicial remedies to uphold the rule of law depends on a culture of legality within the executive branch. When officials are not committed to that culture, more robust equitable remedies are needed to protect the underlying constitutional principles at issue. And courts today should indulge some realism about the nature of the current Administration when crafting remedies to keep it within legal bounds.

Continue Reading in the Full PDF

The post Some Realism About Constitutional Remedies appeared first on Harvard Law Review.
